stem: A data set contains the eight values 4, 7, 9, 12, 14, 17, 19, and 22. The two smallest values, 4 and 7, are removed from the data set. What is the interquartile range (IQR) of the remaining six values?
explanation:
STEP A — Sort the original data set in ascending order: 4, 7, 9, 12, 14, 17, 19, 22.

STEP B — Compute the original mean (for reference):
  Sum = 4 + 7 + 9 + 12 + 14 + 17 + 19 + 22 = 104
  Mean = 104 ÷ 8 = 13

STEP C — Identify the original median (n = 8, even):
  Average of 4th and 5th values = (12 + 14) ÷ 2 = 13

STEP E — Compute the original IQR:
  Lower half (first 4 values): 4, 7, 9, 12 → Q1 = (7 + 9) ÷ 2 = 8
  Upper half (last 4 values): 14, 17, 19, 22 → Q3 = (17 + 19) ÷ 2 = 18
  Original IQR = 18 − 8 = 10

STEP G — After removing 4 and 7, the new data set is: 9, 12, 14, 17, 19, 22 (n = 6).
  Re-sort (already sorted): 9, 12, 14, 17, 19, 22

  New median (n = 6, even): average of 3rd and 4th values = (14 + 17) ÷ 2 = 15.5

  New IQR:
    Lower half (first 3 values): 9, 12, 14 → Q1 = middle value = 12
    Upper half (last 3 values): 17, 19, 22 → Q3 = middle value = 19
    New IQR = 19 − 12 = 7

STEP H — Correct answer = 7, which is choice B. ✓

Key insight: Although both removed values (4 and 7) were below the original Q1 = 8, removing them changes the positions of Q1 and Q3 in the new list. With n = 6, the lower half is {9, 12, 14} and the upper half is {17, 19, 22}, giving Q1 = 12 and Q3 = 19, so IQR = 7 (not 10).

STEP I — Distractor analysis:
  Choice A (5): MISCONCEPTION — wrong quartile positions. The student takes Q1 and Q3 as the 2nd and 4th values of the new 6-item list respectively: Q1 = 12 (2nd value), Q3 = 17 (4th value), IQR = 17 − 12 = 5. This error arises from using the formula for the quartile positions in an odd-n list (positions ⌈n/4⌉ and ⌊3n/4⌋) incorrectly applied to the new list.
  Choice C (10): MISCONCEPTION — IQR-unchanged error. The student recognizes that 4 and 7 are both below the original Q1 = 8 and incorrectly concludes that removing values outside the IQR does not affect the IQR itself. The student reports the original IQR = 10 without recomputing Q1 and Q3 on the new data set.
  Choice D (11): MISCONCEPTION — old Q1 with new Q3. The student correctly recomputes Q3 on the new data set as 19 (the median of {17, 19, 22}) but fails to update Q1, keeping the original Q1 = 8. The student then computes IQR = 19 − 8 = 11, applying a partial update that preserves only the upper quartile.

distractorRationale.A: Wrong quartile positions: the student takes the 2nd value as Q1 (= 12) and the 4th value as Q3 (= 17) in the new 6-item list, computing IQR = 17 − 12 = 5 instead of using the correct lower and upper half medians.
distractorRationale.B: Correct answer: after removing 4 and 7, the new set is {9, 12, 14, 17, 19, 22}; Q1 = median of lower half {9, 12, 14} = 12 and Q3 = median of upper half {17, 19, 22} = 19, giving IQR = 19 − 12 = 7.
distractorRationale.C: IQR-unchanged error: the student notes that both removed values (4 and 7) are below the original Q1 = 8 and incorrectly concludes that removing values outside the IQR leaves the IQR at 10, without recomputing Q1 and Q3 on the modified data set.
distractorRationale.D: Partial-update error: the student correctly recomputes Q3 = 19 on the new data set but retains the original Q1 = 8, computing IQR = 19 − 8 = 11 rather than fully recalculating both quartiles.
